summary: Joel responds to an anonymous Microsoft insider's reaction to his earlier critique of Microsoft .NET. He argues that .NET is not a real product or coherent technical vision but rather marketing blather designed to create the illusion of a grand strategy. He compares it to the previous substance-free 'Windows DNA' campaign. Joel contends that Microsoft's marketing people are allowed to write white papers pretending engineers are building features that are really just wishful thinking.
key_points: Microsoft .NET is criticized as a marketing umbrella term rather than a genuine technical product or vision
The .NET branding follows the same pattern as the failed 'Windows DNA' campaign — repackaging existing products under a buzzword
Microsoft's marketing teams write white papers that overstate what engineering is actually building, creating a gap between vision and reality
money_quotes: It's not that Microsoft doesn't have any new products that they're calling .NET; it's that the concept .NET itself is just marketing blather designed to make it look like there's a grand vision at Microsoft, when all that you have is a handful of upgraded products.
The trouble is, at Microsoft, these marketing guys are allowed to write white papers and pretend that the engineers are really working on that telepathic user interface they asked for.
key_insight: Grand-sounding technology branding like .NET can be hollow marketing that repackages existing products under the illusion of a unified vision.
